source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, AI, global warming, geopolitics
summary: Semiconductors (public and private) are structurally advantaged because AI makes global GDP inexorably more silicon-intensive
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI represents a structural and permanent shift in the silicon-intensity of global GDP, making semiconductors as a category one of his highest-conviction long-term investment themes. His core evidence is a margin/compute-spend comparison: AI companies operate at roughly 40% gross margins with ~50% of revenue flowing to compute costs, versus traditional SaaS businesses at ~85% gross margins with only ~5% going to compute — meaning the AI economy consumes vastly more silicon per dollar of revenue generated. This structural dynamic makes it, in Baker's words, 'hard to bet against semiconductors public and private,' and he expresses particular enthusiasm for venture-stage semiconductor companies, noting that founders in this space are often experienced 50-year-olds with proven track records rather than first-time entrepreneurs. The investment implication is a broad, long-term bullish stance on the semiconductor sector as a whole, spanning public equities and private venture investments, rather than a thesis about any single company or sub-segment. No specific public company is singled out as the primary beneficiary; the thesis is a sector-level structural call.
detail: AI companies have best-case ~40% gross margins with ~50% of revenue going to compute, vs. SaaS at 85% gross margins with ~5% going to compute. This structural shift means AI makes the global economy permanently more silicon-intensive. Baker is bullish on both public and private semis, with particular enthusiasm for venture semis where founders are often experienced 50-year-olds with proven track records.
confidence_evidence: Baker provides specific margin/compute-spend data points and explicitly states 'it's hard to bet against semiconductors public and private' as a capital deployment priority
quote_fragment: AI makes global GDP inexorably more silicon intensive

ticker_audit.reasoning: NVDA is the dominant supplier of AI compute infrastructure and directly benefits from the structural shift toward higher silicon-intensity in the economy. While not the specific subject of this thesis, it is the clearest public-market beneficiary of AI driving more revenue toward compute spend. [RE-AUDIT: Baker's thesis is an explicit sector-level structural call — he says it is 'hard to bet against semiconductors public and private' as a category. He does not specifically name NVDA as a beneficiary of this particular thesis. The summary explicitly states 'No specific public company is singled out as the primary beneficiary.' Including NVDA is an inference about an obvious beneficiary, not a direct connection Baker made in this thesis.]

AMD competes in AI accelerators but Baker's thesis is a broad sector call, not specifically about AMD. AMD's AI GPU share is nascent and its inclusion here appears to be contamination from adjacent semiconductor discussions rather than a clear implication of this thesis.

Broadcom is a major supplier of custom AI ASICs and networking silicon to hyperscalers, directly benefiting from the structural increase in compute spend that Baker describes. [RE-AUDIT: Same reasoning as NVDA. Baker's thesis is a broad sector-level structural argument about silicon-intensity of GDP driven by AI. AVGO is not specifically named or connected to this exact thesis. Its inclusion reflects thematic inference, not a direct statement Baker made linking AVGO to this specific structural claim.]

While memory is part of the semiconductor ecosystem, Baker's thesis focuses on compute spend and silicon-intensity driven by AI inference/training workloads. MU is not unambiguously implied by this specific structural argument and appears to be bleed-in from adjacent memory theses.

Applied Materials is a semiconductor equipment company. Baker's thesis is about the silicon-intensity of the AI economy, not specifically about equipment suppliers. Its inclusion appears to be loose thematic association rather than a direct implication of this thesis.

KLA is a semiconductor equipment/process control company. Same reasoning as AMAT — not a direct or unambiguous beneficiary of the specific structural argument Baker is making about AI compute spend driving silicon-intensity.
Lam Research is a semiconductor equipment company. Same reasoning as AMAT and KLAC — equipment suppliers are loosely related to the theme but not unambiguously implied by this specific thesis.

ASML is a lithography equipment supplier and is not US-listed (it trades as an ADR but is a Dutch company). More importantly, Baker's thesis does not specifically imply equipment suppliers, and ASML is several steps removed from the AI compute-spend dynamic he describes.
